Integration layer (medium): database uniqueness check (test with existing/new emails against a real test database), record storage and retrieval, email service contract (mock or use test endpoint). E2E layer (smallest): 1-2 tests verifying the full registration flow end-to-end. This maximizes fast feedback while ensuring integration points are verified.

questions.question: During a security review, you discover that an API endpoint accepts a JSON body with a nested object: {"user": {"role": "admin"}}. Normal users should only be able to update {"user": {"name": "..."}}. The API uses automatic JSON-to-object mapping. What vulnerability class is this, what are the test cases, and what is the expected fix?

choices.A: Broken authentication; test with expired tokens; fix with token validation
choices.B: SQL injection; test with SQL in the name field; fix with parameterized queries
choices.C: Cross-site scripting; test with script tags; fix with output encoding
choices.D: Mass assignment (also called over-posting); test cases: (1) Normal update with only allowed fields succeeds, (2) Include 'role' field in request body and verify it is rejected or ignored, (3) Include other sensitive fields (is_admin, email_verified, balance) and verify they cannot be modified, (4) Try nested path traversal like {"user": {"permissions": {"admin": true}}}; fix with explicit allowlists of bindable fields

questions.explanation: Mass assignment occurs when APIs automatically bind all incoming JSON fields to internal objects without restricting which fields are writable. An attacker adds extra fields (role, permissions, balance) that the binder maps to the internal model. Test cases must attempt to modify every sensitive field individually and in combination. The fix is an allowlist (whitelist) of fields that can be bound from user input, rejecting or stripping all others. This is OWASP API6: Unrestricted Resource Consumption / Mass Assignment.

questions.question: A performance test shows the following percentile data for API response times: p50=120ms, p75=180ms, p90=350ms, p95=800ms, p99=4200ms. The SLA requires p95 < 500ms. The team proposes adding a CDN cache with 90% hit rate for GET requests (which comprise 70% of traffic). Estimate whether the CDN will meet the SLA and what additional testing is needed.

choices.A: The CDN will solve the problem; no additional testing needed
choices.B: The CDN will make performance worse due to added network hops
choices.C: The CDN will improve GET request performance but POST/PUT/DELETE requests (30% of traffic) are unaffected; the p95 of the remaining uncached traffic may still exceed 500ms because the slow tail is likely concentrated in write operations and cache misses; additional testing must measure p95 specifically for cache-miss and write-operation subsets
choices.D: The SLA should be changed to p99 instead of p95

questions.explanation: A CDN cache with 90% hit rate on GET requests (70% of traffic) means 63% of all requests will be served from cache (very fast). But the p95 threshold is determined by the slowest 5% of requests. Since cache misses (7% of traffic) and write operations (30% of traffic) are unaffected, the 37% of uncached requests likely contain the slow tail. The overall p95 depends on the latency distribution of this 37% subset. Testing must specifically measure percentiles for uncached requests to determine if the SLA is achievable. The CDN helps the median but may not move p95 below 500ms.

questions.question: You are reviewing a test suite for a login function. The suite has 50 test cases and achieves 100% branch coverage. A mutation testing tool introduces 80 mutants and 55 are killed. The unkilled mutants include: 12 boundary condition mutations (changed < to <=), 8 removed null checks, and 5 modified return values. What is the mutation score, what do the survived mutation categories tell you, and what types of test cases are missing?

choices.A: Mutation score 55/80 = 68.75%; increase branch coverage to fix it
choices.B: Mutation score 55/80 = 68.75%; delete the survived mutants since they are equivalent
choices.C: Mutation score 68.75%; all missing tests are about code coverage
choices.D: Mutation score 68.75%; the survived mutations reveal three specific gaps: (1) Boundary assertions are weak -- tests pass the correct side but do not assert failure at the boundary, (2) Null/empty input handling is untested -- the function handles nulls but no test verifies this behavior, (3) Return value assertions are incomplete -- tests may check for success but not verify the specific returned value; add boundary tests, null input tests, and precise return value assertions

questions.explanation: Mutation score is 55/80 = 68.75% (assuming no equivalent mutants). The three categories of survived mutants map directly to missing test types: (1) The 12 survived boundary mutations (< to <=) mean tests do not include values at exact boundaries. (2) The 8 survived null-check removals mean no test passes null inputs to verify they are handled. (3) The 5 survived return value modifications mean tests assert the function succeeds but do not verify the exact return. 100% branch coverage did not catch these because coverage measures execution, not assertion quality.

questions.question: A mobile banking application is being prepared for release. The regression test suite takes 6 hours to execute. The sprint ends Friday and the release must go out Monday morning. Three features were added this sprint: (1) biometric login (high risk, new feature), (2) updated account statement PDF export (medium risk, enhancement), (3) changed button color on settings page (low risk, cosmetic). How should test effort be allocated across the 6 hours?

choices.A: Test only the biometric login since it is the highest risk
choices.B: Allocate approximately 3 hours to biometric login (new security-critical feature requiring extensive testing), 2 hours to PDF export (functional regression and cross-device verification), 30 minutes to button color change (visual verification only), and 30 minutes to core smoke tests (login, transfer, balance check) to verify no regressions in untouched critical paths
choices.C: Run the full automated regression suite for 6 hours regardless of changes
choices.D: 2 hours per feature for equal coverage

questions.explanation: Risk-based test allocation directs effort proportional to risk: biometric login is new, security-critical, and affects authentication (highest risk, most time). PDF export is an enhancement to existing functionality (medium risk, moderate time). Button color is cosmetic with near-zero regression risk (minimal time). The remaining time should cover core smoke tests to ensure critical untouched paths still work. Option C wastes time on low-risk areas; option D ignores regression risk in other features.

questions.question: A defect report states: 'User cannot save profile.' The developer marks it as 'Cannot Reproduce.' Investigation reveals: the defect occurs only when the user's display name contains an emoji (e.g., 'John Rocket'), the backend uses a MySQL column with utf8 encoding (3-byte max), and the emoji requires utf8mb4 (4-byte). What is the root cause category, how should the bug report be improved, and what broader test should be added?

choices.A: Root cause: data encoding mismatch (utf8 3-byte column cannot store 4-byte Unicode characters); improve the bug report by specifying the exact input string, the database encoding configuration, and the error message/truncation behavior; add a systematic Unicode edge case test suite covering: astral plane characters (emoji, CJK extension B), RTL text, combining characters, and maximum-length strings in the character set
choices.B: Root cause: UI bug; improve report by adding a screenshot; add more UI tests
choices.C: Root cause: database corruption; improve report by adding database logs; add database health checks
choices.D: Root cause: network timeout; improve report by adding network traces; add timeout tests

questions.explanation: This is a classic encoding mismatch: MySQL's 'utf8' charset only supports up to 3-byte Unicode characters (BMP), but emoji and many other characters require 4 bytes (supplementary planes). The developer could not reproduce it because they tested with ASCII names. The bug report must include: exact input (with the specific emoji), backend error (truncation or exception), and database column encoding. The broader lesson is that any text field should be tested with 4-byte Unicode characters to catch encoding limitations.

questions.question: You are testing a blue-green deployment. The blue environment (v1.2) is live, and the green environment (v2.0) is staged. v2.0 includes a database migration that adds a column 'preferences JSONB' with a NOT NULL constraint. After switching traffic to green, a critical bug is found and you need to roll back to blue. What is the problem, and what should the test plan have included?

choices.A: Run the migration in reverse automatically
choices.B: Just drop the new column before rolling back
choices.C: The rollback will fail or cause errors because v1.2 code does not know about the new 'preferences' column. If the migration ran on a shared database, v1.2 INSERT statements will fail the NOT NULL constraint. The test plan should have included: (1) Forward compatibility test -- can v1.2 operate against the v2.0 schema? (2) Rollback rehearsal with the actual migration, (3) Verification that migrations are backward-compatible (nullable columns, default values, or separate database per environment)
choices.D: No problem; switch traffic back to blue

questions.explanation: This is a common blue-green deployment trap: if both environments share a database, the schema must be backward-compatible. A NOT NULL column without a default value will cause v1.2 inserts to fail. The test plan should validate: (1) the old code works against the new schema (forward compatibility), (2) actual rollback execution including data state, (3) that all migrations follow backward-compatible patterns (add nullable columns, use defaults, avoid renames/drops until the old version is decommissioned). Simply dropping the column risks data loss.

questions.question: A tester must verify an API's idempotency. The endpoint POST /api/payments accepts an Idempotency-Key header. Design the complete test scenario set. Which set covers all critical idempotency behaviors?

choices.A: Test: (1) Same request+same key returns identical response without creating a duplicate payment, (2) Same key with different request body returns a 409 Conflict or 422 error (not a new payment), (3) Different key with same body creates a new payment (keys are per-request, not per-body), (4) Expired/invalid key handling, (5) Concurrent requests with the same key (race condition on first-write), (6) Key reuse after the original request failed (should the key be reusable or permanently consumed?)
choices.B: Send the same request twice with the same key and verify the second returns the cached response
choices.C: Test only that duplicate requests return HTTP 200
choices.D: Test that the Idempotency-Key header is optional

questions.explanation: Complete idempotency testing must cover: the happy path (duplicate returns same result), the misuse path (same key + different body should be rejected, not silently processed), the independence path (different keys always create new resources), the edge cases (expired keys, concurrent identical requests), and the failure recovery path (what happens when the original request failed -- can the key be reused to retry?). Each of these represents a distinct class of bugs found in production payment systems.

questions.question: A test environment management issue arises: Test environment A has database version 12.1, but production runs 12.4. A test passes in environment A but fails in production. Investigation reveals that a SQL query uses a function whose behavior changed between versions. What process improvements should be implemented?

choices.A: Always test in production directly
choices.B: Implement three process improvements: (1) Environment parity monitoring -- automated checks that test environment versions match production for all components (DB, OS, middleware, libraries), with alerts on drift, (2) Environment configuration as code -- version-locked infrastructure definitions that are updated in lockstep with production changes, (3) Production-equivalent pre-release environment -- at least one test environment that mirrors production configuration exactly, refreshed regularly
choices.C: Add more test cases to cover version differences
choices.D: Pin the production database to version 12.1 to match the test environment

questions.explanation: This is a test environment fidelity issue. The root cause is not the specific function behavior change, but the process that allowed environment drift. The three improvements address detection (automated parity monitoring), prevention (infrastructure-as-code with version locking), and verification (a production-equivalent environment). Testing in production is dangerous for a banking application, adding test cases for version differences is reactive, and holding production back is untenable.

questions.question: You need to test a file upload feature that accepts CSV files. The specification says: max file size 10 MB, max 50,000 rows, columns must be 'name,email,phone', email must be valid format. Design the negative test set that covers the most critical edge cases. Which set is most comprehensive?

choices.A: Upload a valid file and verify success only
choices.B: Test: (1) File exactly 10 MB (boundary), (2) File 10 MB + 1 byte (just over), (3) Exactly 50,000 rows (boundary), (4) 50,001 rows (just over), (5) Missing column header, (6) Extra unexpected column, (7) Swapped column order, (8) Empty file (0 bytes), (9) File with only headers, no data rows, (10) Row with embedded newlines in a quoted field, (11) Row with the CSV delimiter in a field value, (12) UTF-8 BOM at file start, (13) Mixed line endings (CRLF and LF), (14) Valid CSV extension but binary content, (15) Extremely long single field value exceeding column width
choices.C: Upload a file larger than 10 MB and a file with invalid emails
choices.D: Upload files with wrong extensions (.txt, .xlsx) only

questions.explanation: Comprehensive CSV upload negative testing requires boundary tests (size and row limits), structural tests (column count, order, naming), content tests (embedded delimiters, newlines in quoted fields, encoding issues), empty/minimal cases (0 bytes, headers only), and format deception (correct extension with wrong content). Each of these represents a real-world failure mode: users export CSVs from different tools that handle quoting, encoding, and line endings differently. Option A covers only 2 of these 15+ categories.

questions.question: A testing team is transitioning from TMMi Level 2 to Level 3. They currently have per-project test processes but no organizational standard. The team has 8 projects with varying test approaches. What are the key process areas that must be addressed for Level 3, and what is the recommended implementation sequence?

choices.A: Hire external consultants to write the test process documentation
choices.B: Achieve 100% test automation across all projects
choices.C: Address three TMMi Level 3 process areas in sequence: (1) Test Organization -- establish a central test group that defines organizational test policies and standards, (2) Test Training Program -- develop and deliver training on the standardized process to all project teams, (3) Test Process Definition -- create the organizational standard test process (with tailoring guidelines for project-specific adaptation), then pilot on 2-3 projects before organization-wide rollout, using retrospectives to refine the standard
choices.D: Buy a test management tool and mandate its use across all projects

questions.explanation: TMMi Level 3 requires institutionalized, organization-wide test processes. The three key process areas are Test Organization (who owns the standard), Test Training Program (how people learn it), and Test Process Definition (what the standard is). The implementation sequence matters: establish ownership first, then define the process, then train people. Tailoring guidelines allow project-specific adaptation without losing standardization. Piloting on a subset before rollout reduces risk. Tools and automation are Level 4-5 concerns.

questions.question: An API has the following rate limit configuration: 100 requests per minute per API key, with a sliding window. A tester sends 60 requests in the first 30 seconds, waits 15 seconds, then sends 50 requests. At what point should rate limiting kick in, and what is the complete test assertion set?

choices.A: After 100 total requests; assert HTTP 429 on request 101
choices.B: With a sliding window: the 60 requests from seconds 0-30 are all within the current 60-second window. After waiting until second 45 and sending 50 more, the window from second 0-60 contains 110 requests. Rate limiting should trigger on the 101st request (the 41st request of the second batch). Assertions: (1) Requests 1-60 return 200, (2) Requests 61-100 return 200 (first 40 of second batch), (3) Request 101 returns 429 with Retry-After header, (4) After the window slides past second 30 (at second 90), the 60 early requests fall outside the window and capacity is restored
choices.C: The sliding window resets every 60 seconds from the first request; rate limiting triggers at second 60
choices.D: Rate limiting resets after the 15-second pause; all 50 requests should succeed

questions.explanation: A sliding window rate limiter counts requests in the trailing 60-second window from the current moment. At second 45: the window covers seconds -15 to 45, containing all 60 requests from seconds 0-30. Adding 50 more from second 45 onward, the 101st request (60+41) exceeds the 100-request limit within the 60-second window. The key test is window recovery: at second 90, the window covers seconds 30-90, and the 60 requests from seconds 0-30 have aged out, restoring capacity. A fixed window would behave differently, so knowing the algorithm is critical to correct test design.

questions.question: You are the QA lead for a release that includes a breaking API change: v2 of the /users endpoint changes the response schema (nested 'address' object replaced with flat fields). 200 external consumers use v1. The team plans to deprecate v1 in 6 months. Design the complete test and validation strategy for this transition. Which approach covers all critical risks?

choices.A: Test v2 thoroughly and send a deprecation email to consumers
choices.B: Implement a 5-phase test strategy: (1) V2 contract tests -- validate the new schema against documented specification with positive, negative, and edge cases, (2) V1 backward compatibility tests -- verify v1 continues to return the old schema and that no v2 changes leak into v1 responses, (3) Migration validation -- provide a test endpoint where consumers can verify their integrations against v2 before the cutover, (4) Canary deployment -- route 5% of traffic to v2 while monitoring error rates, response schema validation failures, and consumer complaint channels, (5) Deprecation monitoring -- after v1 deprecation notice, track v1 usage metrics to identify consumers who have not migrated before the shutdown deadline, with automated alerts if v1 traffic increases instead of decreasing
choices.C: Run the existing v1 tests against v2 and fix any failures
choices.D: Test v2 only; v1 consumers should have migrated by now

questions.explanation: A breaking API change affecting 200 external consumers requires defense-in-depth testing across the full lifecycle: validating the new version, protecting the old version, enabling consumer migration, controlled rollout, and deprecation tracking. Each phase addresses a distinct risk: v2 correctness, v1 regression, consumer readiness, production stability, and migration completion. Running v1 tests against v2 (option C) would correctly fail since the schema changed, which is expected behavior not a test failure. Ignoring v1 consumers (option D) risks breaking 200 integrations.
